lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: How now, Captain Fluellen! come you from the bridge?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I assure you, there is very excellent services committed at the bridge.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Is the Duke of Exeter safe?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: The Duke of Exeter is as magnanimous as Agamemnon; and a man

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: that I love and honour with my soul, and my heart, and

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: my duty, and my life, and my living, and my uttermost power:

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: he is not—God be praised and blessed!—any hurt in the world; but

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: keeps the bridge most valiantly, with excellent discipline. There is an aunchient

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: lieutenant there at the pridge, I think in my very conscience he

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is as valiant a man as Mark Antony; and he is a

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: man of no estimation in the world; but I did see him

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: do as gallant service.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: What do you call him?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: He is call’d Aunchient Pistol.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: I know him not.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Here is the man.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Captain, I thee beseech to do me favours:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The Duke of Exeter doth love thee well.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Ay, I praise God; and I have merited some love at his hands.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Bardolph, a soldier, firm and sound of heart,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And of buxom valour, hath by cruel fate,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And giddy Fortune’s furious fickle wheel,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: That goddess blind,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: That stands upon the rolling restless stone—

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: By your patience, Aunchient Pistol. Fortune is painted blind, with a muffler

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: afore his eyes, to signify to you that Fortune is blind; and

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: she is painted also with a wheel, to signify to you, which

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is the moral of it, that she is turning, and inconstant, and

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: mutability, and variation: and her foot, look you, is fixed upon a

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: spherical stone, which rolls, and rolls, and rolls: in good truth, the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: poet makes a most excellent description of it: Fortune is an excellent

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: moral.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Fortune is Bardolph’s foe, and frowns on him;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: For he hath stolen a pax, and hanged must a’ be:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: A damned death!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Let gallows gape for dog; let man go free

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And let not hemp his windpipe suffocate:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: But Exeter hath given the doom of death

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: For pax of little price.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Therefore, go speak: the duke will hear thy voice;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And let not Bardolph’s vital thread be cut

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: With edge of penny cord and vile reproach:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Speak, captain, for his life, and I will thee requite.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Aunchient Pistol, I do partly understand your meaning.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Why then, rejoice therefore.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Certainly, aunchient, it is not a thing to rejoice at: for if,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: look you, he were my brother, I would desire the duke to

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: use his good pleasure, and put him to execution; for discipline ought

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: to be used.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Die and be damned! and figo for thy friendship!

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: It is well.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The fig of Spain!

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Very good.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Why, this is an arrant counterfeit rascal; I remember him now; a bawd, a cutpurse.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I’ll assure you, a’ uttered as prave words at the pridge as

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: you shall see in a summer’s day. But it is very well;

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: what he has spoke to me, that is well, I warrant you,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: when time is serve.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Why, ’tis a gull, a fool, a rogue, that now and then

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: goes to the wars, to grace himself at his return into London

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: under the form of a soldier. And such fellows are perfect in

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: the great commanders’ names: and they will learn you by rote where

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: services were done; at such and such a sconce, at such a

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: breach, at such a convoy; who came off bravely, who was shot,

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: who disgraced, what terms the enemy stood on; and this they con

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: perfectly in the phrase of war, which they trick up with new-tuned

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: oaths: and what a beard of the general’s cut and a horrid

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: suit of the camp will do among foaming bottles and ale-washed wits,

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: is wonderful to be thought on. But you must learn to know

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: such slanders of the age, or else you may be marvellously mistook.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I tell you what, Captain Gower; I do perceive he is not

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the man that he would gladly make show to the world he

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is: if I find a hole in his coat, I will tell

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: him my mind. Hark you, the king is coming, and I must

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: speak with him from the pridge.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How now, Fluellen! camest thou from the bridge?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Ay, so please your majesty. The Duke of Exeter has very gallantly

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: maintained the pridge: the French is gone off, look you; and there

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is gallant and most prave passages; marry, th’ athversary was have possession

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: of the pridge; but he is enforced to retire, and the Duke

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: of Exeter is master of the pridge: I can tell your majesty,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the duke is a prave man.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What men have you lost, Fluellen?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: The perdition of th’ athversary hath been very great, reasonable great: marry,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: for my part, I think the duke hath lost never a man,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: but one that is like to be executed for robbing a church,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: one Bardolph, if your majesty know the man: his face is all

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: bubukles, and whelks, and knobs, and flames o’ fire: and his lips

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: blows at his nose, and it is like a coal of fire,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: sometimes plue and sometimes red; but his nose is executed, and his

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: fire’s out.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We would have all such offenders so cut off: and we give

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: express charge, that in our marches through the country, there be nothing

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: compelled from the villages, nothing taken but paid for, none of the

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: French upbraided or abused in disdainful language; for when lenity and cruelty

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: play for a kingdom, the gentler gamester is the soonest winner.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: You know me by my habit.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Well then I know thee: what shall I know of thee?

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: My master’s mind.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Unfold it.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Thus says my king: Say thou to Harry of England: Though we

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: seemed dead, we did but sleep: advantage is a better soldier than

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: rashness. Tell him we could have rebuked him at Harfleur, but that

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: we thought not good to bruise an injury till it were full

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: ripe: now we speak upon our cue, and our voice is imperial:

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: England shall repent his folly, see his weakness, and admire our sufferance.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Bid him therefore consider of his ransom; which must proportion the losses

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: we have borne, the subjects we have lost, the disgrace we have

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: digested; which in weight to re-answer, his pettiness would bow under. For

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: our losses, his exchequer is too poor; for the effusion of our

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: blood, the muster of his kingdom too faint a number; and for

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: our disgrace, his own person, kneeling at our feet, but a weak

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: and worthless satisfaction. To this add defiance: and tell him, for conclusion,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: he hath betrayed his followers, whose condemnation is pronounced. So far my

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: king and master; so much my office.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What is thy name? I know thy quality.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Montjoy.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Thou dost thy office fairly. Turn thee back,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And tell thy king I do not seek him now;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But could be willing to march on to Calais

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Without impeachment: for, to say the sooth,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Though ’tis no wisdom to confess so much

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Unto an enemy of craft and vantage,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My people are with sickness much enfeebled,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My numbers lessened, and those few I have

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Almost no better than so many French;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Who when they were in health, I tell thee, herald,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I thought upon one pair of English legs

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Did march three Frenchmen. Yet, forgive me, God,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That I do brag thus! This your air of France

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Hath blown that vice in me; I must repent.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Go therefore, tell thy master here I am;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My ransom is this frail and worthless trunk,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My army but a weak and sickly guard;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Yet, God before, tell him we will come on,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Though France himself and such another neighbour

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Stand in our way. There’s for thy labour, Montjoy.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Go, bid thy master well advise himself:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If we may pass, we will; if we be hinder’d,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We shall your tawny ground with your red blood

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Discolour; and so, Montjoy, fare you well.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The sum of all our answer is but this:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We would not seek a battle, as we are;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Nor, as we are, we say we will not shun it:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: So tell your master.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: I shall deliver so. Thanks to your highness.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I hope they will not come upon us now.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We are in God’s hands, brother, not in theirs.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: March to the bridge; it now draws toward night:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Beyond the river we’ll encamp ourselves,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And on tomorrow bid them march away.

stage_directions.text: Drum and colours. Enter King Henry, Gloucester and Soldiers.

glosses.definition: a contested bridge on the march to Calais — Exeter holds it
Greek king who led the expedition against Troy — Fluellen's classical flattery of Exeter
ensign-lieutenant — Pistol's rank (pronounced Fluellen-style)
bridge, in Fluellen's Welsh pronunciation — dialect marker, not a typo
Roman general renowned for loyalty and martial skill — another classical flattery from Fluellen
no reputation — Fluellen's accurate judgment of Pistol's social standing, mixed with praise for his soldiering
vigorous courage — 'buxom' originally = pliant, lively
Fortune's spinning wheel — medieval allegorical image; Pistol inflates it with alliteration
Fortune personified as blindfolded — the conventional medieval figure

glosses.phrase: Fortune is painted blind, with a muffler afore his eyes
glosses.definition: Fluellen's scholarly elaboration of the Fortune-allegory Pistol began — she wears a blindfold

glosses.phrase: foot, look you, is fixed upon a spherical stone
glosses.definition: Fortune's traditional emblem — she balances on a sphere that keeps her unstable
a small tablet or medallion (typically metal or wood) kissed during the Mass — Bardolph stole one from a church
the rope of the hangman — 'hemp's windpipe' = be hanged
sentence of death
cheap rope — the hangman's noose
repay; reward
military discipline must be enforced — Fluellen refuses to intercede; Bardolph hangs
fig (Spanish/Italian) — an obscene gesture of contempt, thumb thrust between fingers
same obscene gesture, named as a Spanish import — Pistol at full bombast
absolute; downright (intensifier)
pimp; brothel-keeper
pickpocket
brave, in Fluellen's Welsh accent — dialect marker
dupe; fool (derived from the gull-bird, said to be easily caught)
by memorized routine
small fortification; also, slang for 'head'
dress up; embellish falsely
soldier's uniform worn to impress civilians
drunken, beery wits — tavern audience for Pistol's battle-tales
fault; flaw — Fluellen will watch for a pretext to act
forced to withdraw

glosses.phrase: one that is like to be executed for robbing a church
glosses.definition: Bardolph — his sentence; Fluellen reports it to Henry
pimples, pustules, and swellings — Fluellen's clinical catalogue of Bardolph's inflamed nose
a burning ember — Bardolph's famously red nose
blue, in Fluellen's Welsh accent

glosses.phrase: We would have all such offenders so cut off
glosses.definition: Henry endorses the execution — no favouritism for an old Eastcheap companion
taken from the villages by force — Henry forbids plunder

glosses.phrase: lenity and cruelty play for a kingdom
glosses.definition: when mercy and cruelty are both competing for a kingdom

glosses.phrase: gentler gamester is the soonest winner
glosses.definition: the milder gambler wins fastest — Henry's governance maxim
costume; clothing — Montjoy is identified by his herald's tabard

glosses.phrase: bruise an injury till it were full ripe
glosses.definition: strike at a wound only when it is fully developed — French strategic rationalization
now is our moment to speak — theatrical image
we speak with royal authority
marvel at our patience
think about what he will pay to avoid battle
his small person would buckle under the weight
treasury
the herald's titular name — the office of principal king-of-arms in France, renewed with each officeholder
without hindrance; unopposed
tell the truth
cunning and advantage
this French air — Henry blames Continental atmosphere for his boasting
my weak body — Henry's self-deprecation; 'trunk' = body
an enemy as great as France itself

glosses.phrase: tawny ground with your red blood
glosses.definition: the sunburnt French soil stained red with French blood
Henry's keynote piety — the providentialism that frames his Agincourt victory

annotations.title: Bardolph's execution — Henry's old companion killed, without comment
annotations.body: The execution of Bardolph for stealing a pax (a small altar object) from a French church is one of the play's most pointed structural decisions. Bardolph had been Falstaff's companion throughout the Henry IV plays and one of Prince Hal's Eastcheap drinking-crew; he is an old familiar face, a man Henry knew well in his youth. Fluellen reports the execution almost incidentally: 'one Bardolph, if your majesty know the man: his face is all bubukles, and whelks, and knobs, and flames o' fire...' Henry answers, 'We would have all such offenders so cut off' — a single line, no recognition, no hesitation. Compare Hal's immediate, raw grief when he thought Falstaff was dead in Henry IV Part 1, 5.4. The Henry V who executes Bardolph has successfully eliminated the Prince Hal who knew him. Readers should register the absence of emotion as itself the scene's moral statement. And Henry's follow-on speech forbidding English plunder in the villages — 'the gentler gamester is the soonest winner' — is the same speech that just refused to protect an old companion from the hanging-tree. The morality is consistent, but only if you accept that Henry's conversion from Hal to king was complete.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

annotations.title: Henry's 'plain soldier' frankness with Montjoy
annotations.body: When Montjoy the French herald arrives, Henry's answer is unusually direct: 'My people are with sickness much enfeebled, / My numbers lessened, and those few I have / Almost no better than so many French.' This is a private admission of weakness delivered to the enemy, and it is unexpected. The strategic calculation is one thing: Montjoy will report the English army as a ragged band, inviting the French to attack en masse rather than let the sick English march quietly to Calais. But there is also a rhetorical honesty here that Henry does not display elsewhere in the play. He even apologizes for boasting ('forgive me, God, / That I do brag thus'). The passage prepares us for the Henry of the night before Agincourt — the king who will walk among his men disguised and hear their complaints. The 'plain soldier' pose that dominates the wooing scene is already being rehearsed.
sources: Anne Barton, 'The King Disguised,' in The Triple Bond (1975)
Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)

trials.prompt: How does Henry respond to the news that his old Eastcheap companion Bardolph is to be executed?
options: With a single line endorsing the execution — 'We would have all such offenders so cut off' — showing that Prince Hal has been successfully eliminated from the present king
By sending him back to England in chains

trials.prompt: What does Henry tell Montjoy about the English army's condition?
options: That it is in full health
That the soldiers are enfeebled by sickness and reduced in numbers — a candid admission of weakness, delivered to the enemy
That reinforcements have just arrived from England
That the Dauphin has already surrendered

trials.prompt: Henry's maxim 'the gentler gamester is the soonest winner' (line 92) refers to —
options: A recommendation that soldiers play chess
His policy of forbidding plunder from French villages — strict discipline and payment for supplies, to conciliate the local population and speed the march
Instruction on how to treat horses
